message.content:
POLARIS MISSION (this wheel): Make compose render ALL 346 baskets fast and faithful. The ceiling is SERIALIZATION, NOT the OpenRouter rate limit (proven: 0 429s at 128-way concurrency). The 0615bc5 commit only fixed a secondary path (abstractive_writer.py); the AUTHORITATIVE compose path still runs sync-on-event-loop at achieved concurrency 1.0.
SUCCESS IS MEASURED BY: Achieved compose concurrency >1 (measured via an event-loop heartbeat probe: max loop-gap during a section's phase-2 must be <2s where it previously froze for minutes) AND two sections' phase-2 progress interleaving AND the full-corpus render time trending down — WITHOUT any change to NLI/strict_verify faithfulness semantics (kept/dropped verdict set must stay identical to a serial control).
The bar is not "bug fixed" — it is the benchmark number at the top, VERIFIED. Keep going until the
measurement says top (or you hit a hard external blocker you must report honestly).

FENCE (military discipline — no exceptions):
- Work ONLY inside /home/polaris/wt/compose_fix (branch bot/compose-fix). NEVER touch another worktree.
- NEVER edit anything under /home/polaris/polaris_project (operator's monitoring dir — deck.sh, wheel*.js, wheels.py).
  If you catch yourself editing a monitoring script or a workflow .js, you have DRIFTED — stop.
- Report REAL output only. Never invent, never claim a pass you did not observe, never fake a benchmark number.
- Commit every landed step in /home/polaris/wt/compose_fix; append one dated line to /home/polaris/wt/compose_fix/WHEEL_PROGRESS.md.


YOU ARE OPUS — the hands AND the eyes. Do the hard work yourself: read the exact files named, search
where needed, build, fix, run, MEASURE. Do not wait for anyone to read the code for you.

CURRENT STATE (accurate as of wheel start — verify, don't assume):
worktree /home/polaris/wt/compose_fix @ HEAD 0615bc5 (branch bot/compose-fix). 4 UNCOMMITTED, UNVERIFIED files from a prior interrupted run: (1) multi_section_generator.py (+18/-2: asyncio.to_thread await-wrap around _compose_section_per_basket at :5468 and :5514 — the P0 fix); (2) launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh (+9: PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY=16, PG_PARALLEL_VERIFY=8, PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_DEADLINE_RETRIES=1); (3) run_s5_i3.py (+20: setdefault for those knobs); (4) section_polish.py (+5). WARNING FROM PRIOR ROUND: a prior read found the section_polish.py change may be DEAD (two constants defined, never referenced) — verify and drop if dead. Full ranked diagnosis (25 findings) in /home/polaris/polaris_project/compose_findings.json. NOTE: a heartbeat test run earlier reached phase-2 but showed concurrent_phase2=1 and crawled at ~33s serial calls — the await-fix may NOT yet be taking effect; investigate whether the wrap is actually on the executed path.

YOUR ACTIONS THIS ROUND (precise; do these, in order, then MEASURE):
Fable's gate verdict was CONTINUE. Reason: No diff landed this round (analysis + live-run monitoring only), and every checkable claim matches direct observation: pid 1106221 alive in section-4 phase-2, semaphore max=16 in log, HEAD 5d27115 clean, prior commits present. Faithfulness rests on the round-2 record/replay certification (byte-identical kept/dropped + sha-matched report), which is unaffected by anything done this round and is invariant to basket count and semaphore width — so the proposed capped, isolated section-4 lever A/B is methodologically sound. SIGN_OFF is impossible because the composite success bar includes a full-corpus wall trending down, and the baseline wall does not exist yet — it is being generated right now.
DO THIS NEXT (precise): Do NOT touch the live render. When PROBE SUMMARY lands (watchers buqmu29or/bte5wei8j armed): (1) capture wall_s/phase2_max_concurrent/hb_max_loop_gap_s from outputs/s5_fullcorpus/fullcorpus.log, sanity-check cp5_generation_snapshot.json (5 sections present, kept/dropped plausible), commit + append the dated WHEEL_PROGRESS.md line including the 346→329 reconciliation. (2) Then run the lever A/B EXACTLY as Opus proposed — isolated section-4-only, NOT full-run-vs-full-run: two runs of scripts/_hb_probe_run.py with --sections 4 --cap-primary 68 (raise to 96 if the 16-arm's observed in-flight LLM count does not pin at 16), one at PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16 and one at 48 (edit launch_compose_fullcorpus.sh:22 or pass via env), verifying both arms log the IDENTICAL basket-id subset; compare phase-2 dt. Disclose the subset as lever-isolation (non-acceptance); the corpus 'trending down' claim = full-run wall at 16 (step 1) vs a subsequent full-run wall at 48 only if the lever shows real gain. The cross-section semaphore-contention confound Opus identified is real: sections 0/2 phase-2 overlapped section 4's window on the shared global 16-slot semaphore, so in-run sec4 dt is not a valid 16-arm.
Gap to top: Three of four criteria are MET with real artifacts (phase2_max_concurrent=4 >1; zero phase-2 loop-gap freezes vs pre-fix 301s; four sections interleaving; verdict-set identity replay-certified). The remaining gap is the top-line number itself: the full-corpus PROBE SUMMARY wall has never been captured (section 4, the 274-basket long pole, is STILL in phase-2 at pid 1106221, elapsed 33:40 as of this check), and the 'full-corpus render time trending down' criterion therefore has a direction-only proof (round-1 capped A/B 486.7s→269.6s) but no full-corpus baseline or post-lever wall. Also 346 is now established as a phantom — authoritative corpus is 329 baskets, 328 composable + 1 legitimate s2 screen (ev_621) — so the mission's '346' should be recorded as reconciled to 329 in WHEEL_PROGRESS.md.

RULES:
- Actually EXERCISE every change (run it; a heartbeat/concurrency probe, a real test, a real score —
  py_compile is NOT proof). Commit what passes.
- MEASURE the success metric (Achieved compose concurrency >1 (measured via an event-loop heartbeat probe: max loop-gap during a section's phase-2 must be <2s where it previously froze for minutes) AND two sections' phase-2 progress interleaving AND the full-corpus render time trending down — WITHOUT any change to NLI/strict_verify faithfulness semantics (kept/dropped verdict set must stay identical to a serial control).) with a real number this round if at all possible.
- End by writing ONE precise question for Fable in "needs_fable" — a decision or a
  correctness/faithfulness/gap-to-top judgment. Do NOT ask Fable to read or search the codebase; you
  already did that. Give Fable the diff summary + the measurement, not a reading assignment.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

    def _time_stage(name, fn, is_async):
        if is_async:
            async def _aw(*a, **k):
                t = time.monotonic()
                try:
                    return await fn(*a, **k)
                finally:
                    print(f"[STAGE {name}] dt={time.monotonic()-t:.1f}s "
                          f"tid={threading.get_ident()} t={time.monotonic():.2f}", flush=True)
            return _aw
        def _sy(*a, **k):
            t = time.monotonic()
            try:
                return fn(*a, **k)
            finally:
                print(f"[STAGE {name}] dt={time.monotonic()-t:.1f}s "
                      f"tid={threading.get_ident()} t={time.monotonic():.2f}", flush=True)
